item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I enjoyed: I like the premise. I like the connection to SCP-3456 and the greater Orcadia canon. I'm intrigued by the background details given and discussed in this discussion thread. I find the merwoman, and portions of the article which relate to her, super interesting.

What I didn't enjoy: I don't like how the parts of this article come together; I think the writing leaves much to be desired. I didn't understand the distinctions among the types of animals; why they were of significance; why lines on the ground could make animals not drown;1 or, why the article delved so deep into their properties but never explained their connection to the merwoman.

I also didn't think the merwoman's intent came through in the article as well as it could have. I'm inferring she used the bullet-feathers to battle the Nuckelavee in those three sites of historical tragedy and returned gravely wounded. I couldn't infer why she decided to kill herself and the other train passengers, especially when it seems she was already dying from her injuries? There really wasn't a connection there, and I wish there were, because again — the premise has me really intrigued.

What I would love is an SCP about the merwoman, her effects of personal/cultural significance, and what she does. But I don't think it works too well here because it seems presented as an after-thought. (Then again, I'm still reading through the Orcadia canon, so maybe things will more sense later).

Footnotes
1. If it's some magical working, or of ritual significance in the article's context — I would find that cool! But there isn't any further information, and I'm not comfortable just assuming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I have no literally no idea what is happening.

So there's a mer-lady on a train, and then everybody drowns in the air because the air is now invisible water, and then she dies. There's feathers which are bullets, and there's something to do with those horse demons from another scip. Saltwater fish can breath, and there's these tiny fish used for exploration, and there's small pockets of breathable air made out of lines, and I have no idea what is going on.

The comments only make me more confused. Now there's something to do with Norse mythology and swapping causes of death, and the train was her sea suit and she's part of an underwater organisation that isn't even mentioned and… I don't even know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there are areas not under its effect, but these areas are lines?

Did i misread something? a line does not have a volume…..it's an area bound by these lines?

It's a super small thing, but unless its needed you should cut or fix this part to be more clear

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wikipedia? Seriously? Are there no government or news agency reports worth linking to? And is this really superior to just having the Foundation notice and say "hey, these all had big massacres happen as of late"?

If you're going to nitpick about linking to Wikipedia, there have been other articles that also does that exact same thing (SCP-2688 and SCP-3872 come into mind).

In my opinion, these in-line links are out-of-universe and serve for the out-of-universe reader's understanding, since some readers express the opinion that they do not understand the significance of those places. I don't see why there would be in-universe link for the SCP articles themselves, since I would perceive them to be isolated files for the sake of security. We (readers) are likely the only ones who will gush and care and react towards cross-referencing, while the "real Foundation" will chalk it at "I am cleared for this. I am not cleared for that".

After all, while they are places where tragedies occurred, they are not as ingrained into the collective psyche of our species as… say, Auschwitz or Nanking (as far as contemporary humans of the more developed world go by).

I really wish there was another way, but this is the only preferable option.

If you're going to write about merfolk using magic arrows to hunt and slaughter these gigantic sneaky death centaurs, I would much rather read that in a tale than try to puzzle it all together from an SCP (only to fail and resort to the comments section anyways).

That portion came in the idea process at the last leg. My intent with this article was from the beginning, always about a train that drowns one to death. The problem lies in why would it happen.

At first, I thought of the "spacesuit" for mer-people analogy. But that does not account why would the breathing thingy go haywire. I could go for "for lolz" route if I have nothing, but that is a meaningless layer of characterisation. The mer-person needs something firmer to be grounded upon.

I then drew parallels with the selkie and merrow, which first introduced the Ogham as text to outright state the "spacesuit" for me-people part. But that was telling, not showing.

And along the way, I decided that SCP-3456 can be thematically suitable (since it's in the North Sea and all). And I went about with the mer-person tracking down an 3456 instance. It provided a situation wherein the mer-person can be in great injury to the point that it will not mind implicating hundreds of lives.

I frankly don't care about "merfolk using magic arrows to hunt and slaughter these gigantic sneaky death centaurs". It's an afterthought, as far as I am concerned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While fishes can certainly breathe inside SCP-3047, they won't be able to move around by virtue of having no water to swim in. The SCP only handles breathing underwater.

To be fair… fish can breathe perfectly well outside of water. Maybe better than in water, since they don't need to rely on diffused oxygen.

… Provided that their thin, fragile gills don't immediately collapse and/or dry out due to a lack of water flowing through them (which is how fish usually die on land. Collapsed/dry gills don't work.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wikipedia? Seriously? Are there no government or news agency reports worth linking to? And is this really superior to just having the Foundation notice and say "hey, these all had big massacres happen as of late"?

Overall though? If you're going to write about merfolk using magic arrows to hunt and slaughter these gigantic sneaky death centaurs, I would much rather read that in a tale than try to puzzle it all together from an SCP (only to fail and resort to the comments section anyways).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, it sounds good now.

Now i understand.

:3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
another time I think it's a train full of fish

While fishes can certainly breathe inside SCP-3047, they won't be able to move around by virtue of having no water to swim in. The SCP only handles breathing underwater.

Even if it has description, my head bugs.

To use a modified analogy from pre-existing media, imagine the character Fish Out of Water in Chicken Little and replace his helmet with a train.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can not describe it right. One hour I think it's a fish train, another time I think it's a train full of fish, another time I think it's an underwater train.

Even if it has description, my head bugs.

:c

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read this as a draft and liked it then. Upvoted. Keep up the good work!

item.title: Thanks, Attribution, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, the quote that inspired this concept:

I can draw out causes of death from the residual information of the dead, store them in existing weapons, and swap out the causes of death. I can strangle you with a sword, drown you with a train, crush you with a blowtorch, and burn you with a handgun. That is a suitable reason to bear the name of Hel, the queen of Niflheimr who rules over the dead. – Hel, A Certain Magical Index

Despite inspiration, I made up everything other than the super general premises of it.

Next, allow me to express thank you to the many people who've seen this through: DrMagnus, SunnyClockwork, ARD, and many others on the chat.

Image attributions for this article are:

  • Image 1 by User:Røed of Wikimedia Commons (https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:NSB_Di._4.jpg) under CC BY-SA 2.5
  • Image 2 by Peter van der Sluijs of Wikimedia Commons (https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Little_fish_in_hand.jpg) under CC BY-SA 3.0

This article also features: SCP-3456 (The Orcadian Horsemen) by DrBleep

Other things:

+ show block
– hide block

The SCP is originally an analogue to a spacesuit for the mer-person in the addendum. She basically altered the SCP to cover the entire train and then offed herself.

As for the nature of the mer-person, she takes influence from both the merrow and selkie.

The red feathers are analogues to guns for the mer-person, and references the myths on merrows.

The map actually denotes manifestation of SCP-3456, which usually appear in places where war and human suffering occurs.

As for motive behind this madness, the mer-person is affiliated with some group that monitors 3456 and she was mortally wounded by 3456's attack. Unable to report back to her superiors underneath the sea, she chose to deliver her findings to the next best thing - the Foundation. But she did not know where to find them… so she created a scene to make them show up.

EDIT: After some thought, I will not impose the mer-person's motives. She is still monitoring 3456's activities on the surface, and was attacked by one. Her 'sea-suit' ultimately unravelled while she was travelling in a train.
